policy_statement: Set standards/specifications for a minimum portion of harvested wood products (e.g., mass timber/wood flooring) in certain State-funded/supported new construction and infrastructure when supply chain can meet demand cost-effectively.
mechanism_description: Develop standards and specifications to increase the share of harvested wood products in certain state-funded/supported construction and infrastructure projects, lowering embodied emissions by shifting material use and storing carbon in wood products.
primary_causal_pathway: Public procurement standards → Material substitution (wood for steel/cement) + biogenic carbon storage → ↓ embodied/lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Government-funded construction and infrastructure procurement shifts specified materials toward harvested wood products (e.g., mass timber) through standards/specifications.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: (a) Lower upstream process emissions from producing wood products relative to steel/cement; (b) temporary sequestration of carbon in long-lived wood products; (c) potential additional effects via avoided emissions if wood displaces emissions-intensive materials, contingent on sustainable harvest and supply chain practices.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in embodied/lifecycle GHG emissions associated with public works projects (mitigation).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is lower lifecycle GHG emissions from public construction by substituting wood for higher-embodied-carbon materials (and retaining biogenic carbon in long-lived products).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Procurement-driven material substitution reduces embodied emissions (and can store biogenic carbon), yielding a direct reduction in GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the policy also relates to “resource efficiency” (materials choices) and can have nature-linked elements, the main intended and largest climate effect is GHG mitigation through reduced embodied carbon of construction.  
3) Edge cases applied: Benefits can be reduced or reversed if forestry is unsustainably managed, if land-use change emissions are significant, or if end-of-life leads to rapid oxidation (e.g., burning/decay) without capture; nonetheless, the mechanism’s primary aim and typical dominant effect remains mitigation via embodied-carbon reduction.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy that sets procurement standards to increase harvested wood products specifically to lower embodied emissions (and/or store carbon) shares the same mitigation-dominant pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Further subtyping (e.g., “industrial decarbonization,” “low-carbon materials procurement,” “carbon storage in products”) depends on policy-level evidence (scope, LCA requirements, forestry safeguards) and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net GHG benefit depends on assumptions about sustainable forest management, additionality (does demand increase harvest), carbon accounting time horizons, displacement effects (what materials are actually replaced), and end-of-life handling (reuse vs landfill vs combustion). If a policy is justified primarily for economic development of forestry rather than emissions, the climate classification still follows the dominant physical pathway when it is explicitly tied to embodied-carbon reduction.
key_indicators: “set standards and specifications”; “minimum portion of harvested wood products”; “mass timber or wood flooring”; “new construction”; “State-funded/supported buildings and infrastructure projects”; “supply chain can cost-effectively meet the demand”
co_benefits: Economic development/jobs in NY forest products supply chain; potential support for sustainable forestry/forest management; innovation in construction materials and methods
instance_edge_case_notes: Condition “when New York’s supply chain can cost-effectively meet the demand” adds a feasibility trigger but does not change the instrument type (still a procurement requirement rather than an incentive or target-only measure).
